content.full_text:
CITY OF SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

151 WEST MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
(408) 277-4454

JUN 25 9:50 AM '90

JOAN R. GALLO
City Attorney

June 22, 1990

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request for Advice Letter

Dear Ms. Donovan:

Commissioner Salvatore Caruso has authorized me to seek an
advice letter on his behalf concerning application of conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act to his duties on the
Planning Commission of the City of San Jose.

Facts

Commissioner Caruso is a design professional and the sole owner
of an architectural consulting business whose clients include
persons who propose to develop property located in San Jose. The
Commissioner has sought advice from my office, as to when, if ever,
he may represent clients who have projects before the Planning
Director, Planning Commission or the City Council.

We have provided advice to the Commissioner about representing,
before the Planning Commission and City Council, clients who have
been sources of income of $250 or more in the last twelve (12)
months. This advice is taken from your letter to Mary Jo Levinger,
Town Attorney of Los Gatos. (Levinger Advice Letter, No.
I-88-328.) However, the Levinger letter does not appear to answer
whether the Commissioner may represent a client before our Planning
Director, when the Director is acting as a hearing officer.

The San Jose Planning Director serves in two capacities. His
Department makes recommendations to the Commission regarding
projects before the Commission, and pursuant to the Municipal Code
he is Secretary to the Commission. (See attached San Jose Municipal
Code § 2.21.970.) We have advised the Commissioner that he is

5728j/0172j

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 22, 1990
Page 2


prohibited from contacting the Director or his employees, in their
capacity as Commission "staff," on behalf of a client with respect
to a matter which will come before the Commission, except for
necessary contact concerning the evaluation of any design drawings
he has prepared. Levinger Advice Letter, p. 7.

The Director also serves as a hearing officer and decisionmaker
in various land use proceedings, for example, tentative subdivision
maps and Site Development Permit applications. The Commission has
no budgetary or appointive control over the Director or his
department. Most of the Director's decisions are appealable to the
Commission. However, some decisions are appealable directly to the
City Council.

Questions

1. May Commissioner Caruso represent an applicant before the
Planning Director, or interact with the Director's staff, as to
a land use matter for which the Director will act as a hearing
officer?

2. Does the answer to Question No. 1 above depend on whether the
Director's decision is appealable to the Commission or directly
to the City Council?

3. Would the answer to Question No. 1 be different if an employee
of Commissioner Caruso's consulting firm, rather than the
Commissioner himself, represents the applicant before the
Director or his staff?

If you need more information, please contact me at (408)
277-4454. Thank you in advance for your assistance.

JOAN R. GALLO
City Attorney

By: Debra L. Cauble /pm
DEBRA L. CAUBLE
Sr. Deputy City Attorney

DC:PM:dc

Attachment

JOAN R. GALLO
